segments.title: Light fleet carriers lead Britain's Korean War air effort
segments.summary: Meet the light fleet carriers and aviation-support ships that sustained the Royal Navy's Korean War operations.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke identifies Glory, Ocean, Theseus, and Triumph as the light fleet carriers leading Britain's effort, with Pioneer and Perseus serving as maintenance carriers in the pattern of HMS Unicorn. He traces their short front-line careers and notes how Triumph later remained useful as a forward aviation repair ship. The moment shows that carrier warfare depended on a supporting maintenance system as well as flight decks at sea.

evidence.note: The transcript names the combat and maintenance carriers and outlines their service careers.

segments.title: Colossus-class dimensions expose the cost of aircraft growth
segments.summary: Carrier dimensions and machinery lead into a warning that larger postwar aircraft rapidly consume a ship's nominal air-group capacity.
segments.body: The light fleet carriers were compact 25-knot ships derived from the Unicorn design and could nominally carry up to 52 wartime aircraft. Dr. Clarke stresses that this number depended on the smaller dimensions of 1945 aircraft: jets and later propeller aircraft required more deck, hangar, and handling space. He uses that growth to argue for spare capacity in carrier design, since a larger hull is better able to preserve its air group across successive aircraft generations.

evidence.note: The transcript gives the carriers' principal characteristics and explains how aircraft growth reduces capacity.

segments.title: Light fleet carriers become other navies' fleet carriers
segments.summary: See how British light carrier designs supplied front-line aviation capability to several postwar navies.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke follows the class beyond British service to Australia, Canada, India, France, Argentina, and Brazil. Ships conceived as light carriers could become the principal fleet carriers of smaller navies, with Brazil's Minas Gerais serving into 2001. The discussion also places the early-1950s Royal Navy below the much larger US Navy but ahead of most other naval aviation forces.

evidence.note: The transcript describes export service and compares the scale of postwar naval aviation forces.

segments.title: Seafire FR.47 brings a wartime fighter lineage to Korea
segments.summary: The Seafire FR.47's performance, armament, range, and Korean War service reveal both its potency and its carrier limitations.
segments.body: The FR.47 was closely related to the late-mark Spitfire and served with 800 Naval Air Squadron aboard HMS Triumph during the Malayan Emergency and Korean War. Dr. Clarke emphasizes its fast climb, four 20 mm cannon, and bomb-carrying ability, but regards it as an aircraft crews would replace if a better option were available. Its limited internal-fuel combat radius helps explain why the Fleet Air Arm needed a more robust postwar carrier fighter.

evidence.note: The transcript identifies the FR.47's service, performance, weapons, and range.

segments.title: Fairey Firefly combines strike, night, and anti-submarine roles
segments.summary: Firefly performance and payload figures illustrate the value of a versatile two-seat carrier aircraft in Korea-era operations.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke presents the Firefly as a much-improved answer to the operational needs once met by the Fulmar. Its radar and radio equipment supported night flying, while cannon, rockets, and bombs allowed attack, air-suppression, and anti-submarine missions. The tradeoff was a slower climb than the Seafire, balanced by useful range and much broader mission equipment.

evidence.note: The transcript details the Firefly's range, armament, radar, and multiple operational roles.

segments.title: Sea Fury proves a piston fighter still matters in Korea
segments.summary: Ground attack, artillery spotting, patrols, and a MiG-15 victory show the Sea Fury's breadth at the edge of the jet age.
segments.body: The Royal Navy adopted the Sea Fury because its aging Seafires lacked endurance and durability, even as the RAF chose to wait for jets. In Korea, British and Australian Sea Furies flew ground attack, combat air patrol, and artillery-spotting missions. Dr. Clarke recounts the August 1952 engagement in which Sea Furies from HMS Ocean fought MiG-15s and received credit for at least one jet fighter destroyed, while noting uncertainty over the individual pilot responsible.

evidence.note: The transcript explains Sea Fury adoption, Korean War roles, and the disputed attribution of a MiG-15 shootdown.

segments.title: Sea Vampire training reveals the straight-deck jet problem
segments.summary: The Royal Navy's jet-induction squadron trained with Sea Vampires while Korean War carriers still fought with piston aircraft from straight decks.
segments.body: The Fleet Air Arm held Sea Vampires in a 700-series training and trials unit rather than sending them into combat; 800-series squadrons remained the operational formations. Their presence nevertheless exposed the difficulty of operating faster-approaching jets from the long straight decks of light fleet carriers. Quoting Caspar John, Dr. Clarke contrasts the new communist jet threat with the piston aircraft carried by Triumph, Theseus, Glory, and Ocean, framing Korea as an argument for better aircraft and carriers.

evidence.note: The transcript distinguishes training and combat squadrons and links jet approach speeds to straight-deck limitations.

segments.title: Jet speed collapses radar warning time
segments.summary: A simple warning-time comparison explains why fleets had to push radar coverage and combat air patrols farther from the carrier.
segments.body: An aircraft detected 80 miles away at 400 mph gives a fleet roughly twelve minutes to react; at 1,200 mph, the warning shrinks to about three minutes. The slower case allows fighters to scramble or a combat air patrol to intercept, while the faster attacker may pass the patrol before it can engage. Dr. Clarke uses the comparison to show that jet speed changed fleet geometry: detection and defensive fighters had to move outward from the carrier.

evidence.note: The transcript compares warning times and explains the resulting need to push combat air patrols outward.

segments.title: Saunders-Roe SR.A/1 explores a carrier-independent jet fighter
segments.summary: The SR.A/1 flying-boat fighter shows how seriously Britain explored unconventional ways to deploy jets at sea.
segments.body: The Saunders-Roe SR.A/1 offered a jet fighter able to operate from water without a carrier flight deck. Testing showed that water ingestion did not automatically flame out its engines, but the concept lacked an institutional sponsor: the Royal Navy would not fund it and the RAF did not need it. The design is therefore a revealing technological branch rather than the solution to the fleet air-defence problem posed earlier.

evidence.note: The transcript describes the flying-boat jet concept, engine behavior, and lack of service funding.
